properties.label: 02:28 (UTC+8) extra arc (CAPTIO)
properties.name: 02:28 (UTC+8) extra satellite arc (CAPTIO 'Arc-1.2')
properties.plain: This teal circle is an 8th distance ring, beyond the seven official arcs. Just after 02:28 (UTC+8), about three minutes after its satellite phone reconnected at 02:25 (UTC+8), the aircraft sent another automatic status message. The signal's round-trip time fixes how far the plane was from the satellite, and every point at that distance forms this ring, so the aircraft was somewhere along it at that moment. The measured distance is almost identical to the 02:25 (UTC+8) reading, meaning the plane had barely moved in those three minutes. The official investigation folded this message into the first arc; the CAPTIO researchers keep it as a separate check on their route, naming it 'Arc-1.2'.
properties.messageType: 0x62 - Acknowledge User Data (R-channel), within the 18:25:27 log-on / data-exchange session
properties.reason: The BTO here (12480 us) is normal and usable, essentially the same ring as the 02:25:27 (UTC+8) log-on (12520 us corrected) ~2m48s earlier. The main reason it is not a separate arc is redundancy: it lies inside the SAME 02:25 (UTC+8) log-on handshake, so Ashton et al./ATSB/DSTG treat the 02:25/02:28 (UTC+8) messages as one 'first arc' rather than two. Separately, Ashton et al. 2015 judged it prudent to discount the BFO measurements across that log-on window (18:25:34-18:28:15 UTC in the paper's own notation) as not fully understood, possibly a frequency transient during the log-on sequence; the value CAPTIO reintroduce as 'Arc-1.2' comes from that discounted window. It is a BFO discount: Ashton's explicit anomalous-BTO exclusions are only the 18:25:34 and 00:19:37 UTC messages (not this one), and the 02:28 (UTC+8) BTO itself is not anomalous. Not a log-on request (0x62, not 0x10), so no 4600 us log-on correction applies: corrected BTO = raw.

citation.label: Blelly & Marchand (CAPTIO), Table 10; released Inmarsat log (davetaz CSV)
